name: Numbers to 1,000,000
subject: Mathematics
curriculum_reference: NC Y5 Number — Number and Place Value: read, write, order and compare numbers to at least 1,000,000 and determine the value of each digit
NC Y5 Number — Number and Place Value: interpret negative numbers in context, count forwards and backwards with positive and negative whole numbers including through zero
representations: number line with intervals of 10,000 and 100,000
fluency_targets: Read and write numbers to 1,000,000 in numerals and words
Identify the value of any digit in a number up to 1,000,000
Round any number up to 1,000,000 to the nearest 10, 100, 1,000, 10,000 or 100,000
Count forwards and backwards through zero with negative numbers
reasoning_prompts: A number rounds to 450,000 to the nearest 10,000. What is the largest number it could be? What is the smallest?
Explain why -3 is less than -1 even though 3 is greater than 1.
Two numbers both have a 7 in them. In one number the 7 is worth 70,000 and in the other it is worth 700. What could the numbers be?
application_contexts: Reading populations of countries and cities from data tables
Interpreting temperatures above and below zero in weather reports
Comparing distances between planets in the solar system

name: Addition and Subtraction Strategies
subject: Mathematics
curriculum_reference: NC Y5 Number — Addition and Subtraction: add and subtract whole numbers with more than 4 digits, including using formal written methods (columnar addition and subtraction)
NC Y5 Number — Addition and Subtraction: use rounding to check answers to calculations and determine, in the context of a problem, levels of accuracy
manipulatives: place value counters for regrouping demonstrations
representations: number line for estimation and checking
fluency_targets: Use columnar addition and subtraction fluently with numbers exceeding 4 digits
Add and subtract mentally with increasingly large numbers using known facts and place value
Use inverse operations to check answers
reasoning_prompts: The answer to an addition is 503,217. One of the numbers is 287,653. What is the other number? How do you know?
Amir says that 456,789 - 299,999 is easy to work out mentally. Do you agree? Explain your reasoning.
Find two 5-digit numbers that have a difference of exactly 10,000. How many different pairs can you find?
application_contexts: Calculating total distances on multi-leg journeys
Working out remaining budgets after multiple purchases
Finding differences in population figures between census years

name: Multiplication and Division
subject: Mathematics
curriculum_reference: NC Y5 Number — Multiplication and Division: multiply numbers up to 4 digits by a one or two-digit number using a formal written method, including long multiplication
NC Y5 Number — Multiplication and Division: divide numbers up to 4 digits by a one-digit number using the formal written method of short division and interpret remainders appropriately
NC Y5 Number — Multiplication and Division: identify multiples and factors, including finding all factor pairs of a number, and common factors of two numbers
manipulatives: place value counters for division modelling
representations: bar model for division with remainders
fluency_targets: Recall all multiplication facts to 12 x 12 with automaticity
Use long multiplication for up to 4-digit by 2-digit calculations
Use short division for up to 4-digit by 1-digit calculations
Identify all factor pairs of numbers to 100
Recognise and use square numbers and cube numbers
reasoning_prompts: A number has exactly 3 factor pairs. What could the number be? Can you find more than one answer?
Explain why all square numbers have an odd number of factors.
When you divide a 4-digit number by 7, the remainder is 6. What could the number be? What is the smallest such number?
Is it possible for a prime number to also be a square number? Convince me.
application_contexts: Calculating costs for school events with multiple items and quantities
Dividing quantities equally and interpreting what to do with remainders in context
Finding common factors when grouping children for activities

name: Fractions, Decimals and Percentages
subject: Mathematics
curriculum_reference: NC Y5 Number — Fractions: compare and order fractions whose denominators are all multiples of the same number
NC Y5 Number — Fractions: add and subtract fractions with the same denominator and denominators that are multiples of the same number
NC Y5 Number — Fractions: read and write decimal numbers as fractions
NC Y5 Number — Fractions: recognise the per cent symbol (%) and understand that per cent relates to 'number of parts per hundred'
representations: number line with fractions and decimals
fluency_targets: Identify equivalent fractions by multiplying or dividing numerator and denominator
Add and subtract fractions with related denominators
Convert between common fractions, decimals and percentages (e.g. 1/2 = 0.5 = 50%)
Read, write and order decimals to three decimal places
reasoning_prompts: 3/5 of a number is 45. What is 4/5 of the number? Explain how you worked it out.
Which is greater: 3/8 or 5/12? How can you compare them without drawing a picture?
A shop reduces prices by 10%. Would you rather have 10% off a £60 item or 20% off a £35 item? Explain your reasoning.
Tom says 0.45 is bigger than 0.8 because 45 is bigger than 8. Is he correct? Explain why.
application_contexts: Comparing sale prices using percentage discounts
Calculating fractional quantities in recipes when scaling
Converting between metric units using decimal notation

name: Volume of Cuboids
subject: Mathematics
curriculum_reference: NC Y5 Measurement: estimate volume and capacity
NC Y5 Measurement: use the properties of rectangles to deduce related facts and find missing lengths and angles
representations: layered diagrams showing volume as layers of area
fluency_targets: Estimate the volume of cubes and cuboids using standard units (cm³ and m³)
Calculate volume by counting unit cubes
Begin to connect volume with multiplication of three dimensions
reasoning_prompts: Two cuboids have the same volume. One is 2cm x 3cm x 6cm. What could the dimensions of the other one be?
A cuboid made from 24 centimetre cubes has a height of 2cm. What could its length and width be? Find all possibilities.
Explain why you cannot find the volume of a shape just by knowing its height.
application_contexts: Comparing volumes of packaging and containers
Estimating how many small boxes fit inside a large box
Filling containers with unit cubes to measure capacity

name: Geometry: Angles and Shapes
subject: Mathematics
curriculum_reference: NC Y5 Geometry — Properties of Shapes: know angles are measured in degrees: estimate and compare acute, obtuse and reflex angles
NC Y5 Geometry — Properties of Shapes: identify angles at a point and one whole turn (total 360°), angles at a point on a straight line and half a turn (total 180°)
manipulatives: folding paper strips for angle construction
representations: diagrams showing angles on a straight line and at a point
fluency_targets: Measure angles accurately using a protractor
Identify and name acute, obtuse, reflex and right angles
Calculate missing angles on a straight line (sum = 180°)
Calculate missing angles at a point (sum = 360°)
reasoning_prompts: Two angles on a straight line are equal. What is each angle? What if there were three equal angles?
An angle is reflex. Its supplement (the angle that completes a full turn) is 85°. What is the reflex angle?
Is it possible to have a triangle with a reflex angle? Convince me why or why not.
application_contexts: Measuring angles in real-world structures and designs
Calculating turn angles for programming directional robots
Reading compass bearings and map directions

name: Reflections and Translations
subject: Mathematics
curriculum_reference: NC Y5 Geometry — Position and Direction: identify, describe and represent the position of a shape following a reflection or translation, using the appropriate language, and know that the shape has not changed
fluency_targets: Reflect shapes in horizontal and vertical mirror lines on a coordinate grid
Translate shapes by given vectors described in words or on a grid
Identify that reflected and translated shapes are congruent to the original
reasoning_prompts: After a reflection, the point (2, 5) maps to (8, 5). Where is the mirror line?
A shape is translated 3 right and 2 up. What single translation would return it to its original position?
Convince me that a reflection changes the orientation of a shape but a translation does not.
application_contexts: Identifying reflective symmetry in art and design patterns
Describing movements on maps and coordinate grids
Designing tessellation patterns using translations

name: Graphs, Tables and Timetables
subject: Mathematics
curriculum_reference: NC Y5 Statistics: solve comparison, sum and difference problems using information presented in a line graph
NC Y5 Statistics: complete, read and interpret information in tables, including timetables
fluency_targets: Read and plot line graphs with appropriate scales
Interpret intermediate values on a line graph
Read and use timetables to plan journeys and calculate durations
Solve two-step problems using data from tables
reasoning_prompts: A line graph shows temperature rising steeply then levelling off. What real-world situation could this represent?
The train timetable shows a journey of 45 minutes. The train departs at 09:47. What time does it arrive? Explain how you worked this out.
Two line graphs show sales for two shops. Shop A has a steeper line. Does this mean Shop A sold more? Explain.
application_contexts: Planning journeys using real bus or train timetables
Recording and interpreting science experiment data on line graphs
Comparing data across categories using tables

name: Place Value to 10,000,000
subject: Mathematics
curriculum_reference: NC Y6 Number — Number and Place Value: read, write, order and compare numbers up to 10,000,000 and determine the value of each digit
NC Y6 Number — Number and Place Value: round any whole number to a required degree of accuracy
NC Y6 Number — Number and Place Value: use negative numbers in context, and calculate intervals across zero
manipulatives: place value counters for conceptual introduction
fluency_targets: Read, write and order numbers to 10,000,000
Identify the value of any digit in numbers to 10,000,000
Round any whole number to a required degree of accuracy
Calculate intervals across zero with negative numbers
reasoning_prompts: A number rounds to 3,500,000 to the nearest 100,000. What is the range of numbers it could be?
The temperature at midnight is -4°C. By noon it is 9°C. What is the temperature rise? Explain how you calculated this.
Write a 7-digit number where the digit 5 has a value 1,000 times greater than the digit 5 in the number 3,524.
application_contexts: Interpreting national and global population statistics
Working with distances in astronomy and geography
Reading and comparing temperature data including sub-zero values

name: Four Operations
subject: Mathematics
curriculum_reference: NC Y6 Number — Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division: multiply multi-digit numbers up to 4 digits by a two-digit whole number using the formal written method of long multiplication
NC Y6 Number — Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division: divide numbers up to 4 digits by a two-digit whole number using the formal written method of long division
NC Y6 Number — Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division: perform mental calculations, including with mixed operations and large numbers
NC Y6 Number — Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division: use their knowledge of the order of operations to carry out calculations involving the four operations
manipulatives: place value counters for conceptual introduction to long division
fluency_targets: Use long multiplication for up to 4-digit by 2-digit calculations
Use long division for up to 4-digit by 2-digit calculations and express remainders as fractions or decimals
Perform mental calculations using known facts and place value with confidence
Apply BIDMAS (order of operations) to multi-operation calculations
Identify common factors, common multiples and prime numbers
reasoning_prompts: Insert brackets into 3 + 5 x 2 - 1 to make the answer 15. Can you make it equal other values?
A 4-digit number divided by a 2-digit number gives an answer with no remainder. The answer is between 30 and 40. What could the numbers be?
Explain why you cannot divide by zero. Use a multiplication fact to help your explanation.
Which gives a larger answer: 4,826 x 37 or 4,837 x 26? Can you tell without fully calculating both? Explain your reasoning.
application_contexts: Multi-step word problems involving money, distance and time
Calculating unit costs for comparison shopping
Interpreting and solving problems involving timetables and schedules

name: Fractions, Decimals and Percentages
subject: Mathematics
curriculum_reference: NC Y6 Number — Fractions: use common factors to simplify fractions; use common multiples to express fractions in the same denomination
NC Y6 Number — Fractions: add and subtract fractions with different denominators and mixed numbers, using the concept of equivalent fractions
NC Y6 Number — Fractions: multiply simple pairs of proper fractions, writing the answer in its simplest form
NC Y6 Number — Fractions: recall and use equivalences between simple fractions, decimals and percentages
representations: bar model for fraction of amounts
fluency_targets: Simplify fractions using common factors
Add and subtract fractions with unrelated denominators
Multiply pairs of proper fractions and simplify the result
Divide proper fractions by whole numbers
Fluently convert between fractions, decimals and percentages for common equivalences
reasoning_prompts: 2/3 + 3/4 is close to which whole number? How do you know without calculating exactly?
Why is 1/3 x 1/4 smaller than both 1/3 and 1/4? Does multiplying always make numbers bigger?
A price increases by 10% and then decreases by 10%. Is the final price the same as the original? Prove it.
Put these in order: 3/8, 40%, 0.37. Explain your method.
application_contexts: Calculating percentage discounts in shopping contexts
Scaling recipe ingredients using fraction multiplication
Interpreting pie charts where segments are given as fractions or percentages

name: Ratio and Proportion
subject: Mathematics
curriculum_reference: NC Y6 Ratio and Proportion: solve problems involving the relative sizes of two quantities where missing values can be found by using integer multiplication and division facts
NC Y6 Ratio and Proportion: solve problems involving unequal sharing and grouping using knowledge of fractions and multiples
manipulatives: multilink cubes for building ratio models
fluency_targets: Use ratio notation (e.g. 3:1) and understand its meaning
Solve simple ratio problems using multiplication facts
Scale quantities up and down using ratio
Connect ratio to fractions (e.g. in the ratio 2:3, the first part is 2/5 of the total)
reasoning_prompts: Orange squash is mixed in the ratio 1:4 (squash to water). If I use 350ml of water, how much squash do I need? What if I want a stronger drink?
In a class, the ratio of boys to girls is 3:5. There are 24 children. How many boys? How many girls? What fraction of the class are boys?
A recipe for 6 people uses 300g of rice. Explain two different methods for finding how much rice is needed for 9 people.
application_contexts: Mixing paint colours in given ratios
Scaling recipes for different numbers of people
Interpreting map scales to calculate real distances

name: Algebra
subject: Mathematics
curriculum_reference: NC Y6 Algebra: use simple formulae
NC Y6 Algebra: generate and describe linear number sequences
NC Y6 Algebra: express missing number problems algebraically
NC Y6 Algebra: find pairs of numbers that satisfy an equation with two unknowns
representations: sequence diagrams with term-to-term and position-to-term rules
fluency_targets: Use letters to represent unknowns and variables
Substitute values into simple formulae
Generate terms of a linear sequence given a rule
Find the nth term rule for simple linear sequences
Enumerate possibilities for equations with two unknowns
reasoning_prompts: If 3a + 7 = 22, what is the value of a? Explain your method step by step.
Find all pairs of whole numbers where x + y = 12 and x > y. How do you know you have found them all?
The perimeter of a regular pentagon is 5n. If n = 3.5, what is the perimeter? What does n represent?
A sequence starts 3, 7, 11, 15... Will the number 100 be in this sequence? How can you tell without listing every term?
application_contexts: Writing formulae for perimeters and areas of shapes
Using formulae to convert between units (e.g. miles and kilometres)
Solving real-world problems by forming and solving equations

name: Measurement: Area and Volume
subject: Mathematics
curriculum_reference: NC Y6 Measurement: recognise that shapes with the same areas can have different perimeters and vice versa
NC Y6 Measurement: calculate the area of parallelograms and triangles
NC Y6 Measurement: recognise when it is possible to use the formulae for area and volume of shapes
NC Y6 Measurement: calculate, estimate and compare volume of cubes and cuboids using standard units
manipulatives: cut-out parallelograms and triangles for rearrangement
representations: triangle as half a rectangle/parallelogram diagrams
comparison diagrams for same area/different perimeter
fluency_targets: Calculate the area of parallelograms using base x height
Calculate the area of triangles using 1/2 x base x height
Calculate the volume of cubes and cuboids using length x width x height
Convert between mm², cm², m² and km² as appropriate
reasoning_prompts: A triangle and a parallelogram share the same base and height. What is the relationship between their areas? Will this always be true?
Design two different rectangles with an area of 36 cm². Which has the smaller perimeter? What pattern do you notice?
A cuboid has a volume of 60 cm³. One edge is 3 cm and another is 4 cm. What is the third edge? How do you know?
application_contexts: Calculating the area of floors for tiling or carpet fitting
Comparing volumes of packaging to find the best value
Estimating the area of irregular real-world shapes by decomposition

name: Geometry: Shapes and Angles
subject: Mathematics
curriculum_reference: NC Y6 Geometry — Properties of Shapes: draw 2-D shapes using given dimensions and angles
NC Y6 Geometry — Properties of Shapes: recognise, describe and build simple 3-D shapes, including making nets
NC Y6 Geometry — Properties of Shapes: compare and classify geometric shapes based on their properties and sizes and find unknown angles in any triangles, quadrilaterals and regular polygons
NC Y6 Geometry — Properties of Shapes: illustrate and name parts of circles, including radius, diameter and circumference
fluency_targets: Draw accurate 2-D shapes given dimensions and angles using protractor and ruler
Find unknown angles in triangles (angle sum = 180°) and quadrilaterals (angle sum = 360°)
Calculate interior angles of regular polygons
Name and identify radius, diameter and circumference of circles
Construct nets for common 3-D shapes
reasoning_prompts: The angles of a triangle are in the ratio 1:2:3. What are the angles? What type of triangle is it?
A regular polygon has interior angles of 120°. How many sides does it have? Explain your method.
The diameter of a circle is 14 cm. What is the radius? A pupil says the circumference must be exactly 42 cm. Are they correct? Why?
Can you draw a quadrilateral with exactly 3 right angles? Explain.
application_contexts: Designing packaging using nets of 3-D shapes
Constructing accurate scale drawings and plans
Identifying geometric properties in architecture and design

name: Coordinates and Transformations
subject: Mathematics
curriculum_reference: NC Y6 Geometry — Position and Direction: describe positions on the full coordinate grid (all four quadrants)
NC Y6 Geometry — Position and Direction: draw and translate simple shapes on the coordinate plane, and reflect them in the axes
representations: reflection diagrams across x-axis and y-axis
fluency_targets: Plot and read coordinates in all four quadrants
Reflect shapes in the x-axis and y-axis on a coordinate grid
Translate shapes on a coordinate grid and describe the translation
Identify the coordinates of vertices after reflection or translation
reasoning_prompts: A point is at (3, -2). After a reflection in the y-axis, where is the image? What about after a reflection in the x-axis?
A square has vertices at (1,1), (1,4), (4,4) and (4,1). After a translation of (-5, -3), what are the new coordinates? Is the shape still a square?
Two shapes are reflections of each other. One vertex is at (-3, 5) and its image is at (3, 5). What is the mirror line? How do you know?
application_contexts: Programming movements on a coordinate grid
Reading and plotting positions using all four quadrants on maps and plans
Creating symmetrical designs using coordinate reflections

name: Statistics
subject: Mathematics
curriculum_reference: NC Y6 Statistics: interpret and construct pie charts and line graphs and use these to solve problems
NC Y6 Statistics: calculate and interpret the mean as an average
manipulatives: multilink cubes for demonstrating mean as levelling
fluency_targets: Construct and interpret pie charts
Calculate the mean of a set of data
Interpret line graphs including reading intermediate values
Construct and interpret two-way tables
Use all types of statistical representation to solve comparison and difference problems
reasoning_prompts: The mean of five numbers is 12. Four of the numbers are 10, 8, 15 and 13. What is the fifth number?
A pie chart shows that 1/4 of pupils walk to school. There are 32 pupils in the class. How many walk? How many do not walk?
Two classes both have a mean test score of 75%. Does this mean the classes performed equally well? What other information would help you compare them?
Can the mean of a set of numbers ever be higher than the largest number? Explain why or why not.
application_contexts: Analysing real datasets from geography or science investigations
Comparing performance data using mean averages
Presenting survey results using appropriate chart types
